nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes and protected reef faces with moderate light and indirect flow

decorPreferences: open space (at least 15 cm) on all sides from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented benefiting from supplemental target feeding 1–3 times per week with small meaty foods such as enriched brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or commercial LPS coral foods. Tentacles tend to extend most strongly at night, which has been reported to be the most effective feeding window.

goodWith: clownfish (with caution — may attempt to host the coral as a surrogate anemone)
other Euphyllia / Fimbriaphyllia species (Octospawn, Frogspawn, Hammer, and Torch can generally touch each other without harm)
non-stinging soft corals placed well out of sweeper range
avoidWith: other LPS corals placed within 15 cm
SPS corals placed within sweeper range
zoanthids and mushroom corals placed near the colony
peppermint shrimp (have been known to pick at polyps)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Laszlo (1536x2048). Sharp, whole live colony in an aquarium showing the branching multi-lobed tentacles with white bulb tips characteristic of Fimbriaphyllia yaeyamensis (labeled under accepted synonym Euphyllia yaeyamaensis). Only CC-friendly live photo found; natural tan/brown coloration rather than the neon green/purple morph.

summary: The Octospawn Coral (Fimbriaphyllia yaeyamaensis) is a large polyp stony coral recognized by its thick, multi-lobed tentacles with a distinctive branching 'octo' pattern, typically in vivid green and purple. A close relative of frogspawn and hammer corals, it is generally considered an intermediate coral requiring moderate light and gentle, indirect flow.
careNotes:
Taxonomy note: Fimbriaphyllia yaeyamaensis was reclassified out of Euphyllia in 2017 based on molecular phylogenetic work. The older name Euphyllia yaeyamaensis remains widely used across retail listings, and the coral is often sold under the trade name 'Thick Branched Frogspawn' rather than 'Octospawn' depending on the retailer.

Distinguishing it from frogspawn: The Octospawn and Frogspawn (Fimbriaphyllia divisa) are closely related and their husbandry is functionally identical, but the two are reliably distinguished by tentacle morphology. Frogspawn tentacles end in characteristic grape-cluster bulb tips, while Octospawn tentacles are thicker and terminate in a multi-lobed 'octo' branching pattern — the source of the trade name. The two are sold and priced as distinct corals in the aquarium market.

Lighting: Generally advised to keep in moderate light, around 80–150 PAR (a measure of photosynthetically useful light reaching the coral).

Flow: Moderate, indirect, non-laminar flow is consistently recommended. Direct laminar flow has been reported to damage the polyp tissue and cause prolonged retraction. The goal is a gentle back-and-forth swaying motion of the tentacles rather than a steady blast from one direction.

Placement: Generally advised to place in the middle or lower zone of the aquarium, anchored securely to stable rockwork. Open clearance of at least 15 cm on all sides from non-Euphyllia neighbors is strongly recommended given the reach of the sweeper tentacles.

Aggression / Allelopathy: Octospawn has sweeper tentacles capable of stinging neighboring non-Euphyllia LPS corals, SPS frags, zoanthids, and mushroom corals, with a reported reach of commonly up to 15 cm, often extended at night. Like other Euphyllia and Fimbriaphyllia corals, it has been documented releasing chemical compounds into the water column — a process called allelopathy — that can stress non-Euphyllia LPS elsewhere in the system, particularly in smaller or lower-flow aquariums. Activated carbon and regular water changes are commonly recommended when keeping this species alongside sensitive LPS corals.

Euphyllia-friendly placement exception: Octospawn, Frogspawn (Fimbriaphyllia divisa), Hammer (Fimbriaphyllia ancora), Branching Hammer (Fimbriaphyllia paraancora), and Torch (Euphyllia glabrescens) corals have generally been observed to coexist without harming each other even when tentacles make contact. This cross-Euphyllia tolerance is widely reported in the hobby, though individual colonies may vary.

Feeding: Supplemental target feeding is commonly recommended even though the coral is primarily photosynthetic. Offering enriched brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or commercial LPS foods 1–3 times per week has been reported to improve growth rate and coloration. Feeding tends to be most effective at night when tentacles are fully extended; directing food to extended polyps with a pipette or turkey baster is preferred over broadcast feeding.

Brown Jelly Disease: Like other Euphyllia and Fimbriaphyllia corals, Octospawn is considered susceptible to brown jelly disease — a bacterial infection that presents as a rapidly spreading brown mucus over the coral tissue.

Fragging: Propagation by fragging is practiced in captivity. Bone cutters are used to separate individual branches at the skeletal junction, similar to the method used for branching frogspawn. Resulting frags begin small and expand over months to full head size.

Water chemistry stability: Like all large polyp stony corals, Octospawn consumes calcium and alkalinity as it builds its skeleton. Maintaining stable alkalinity (avoiding swings greater than 1 dKH per day) and adequate calcium (400–450 ppm) and magnesium (1250–1350 ppm) is generally advised to support growth and prevent bleaching.
breedingNotes: Fragging — the mechanical division of branched colonies — is the primary captive propagation method for Octospawn. Individual branches are cut at the skeletal junction with bone cutters, and the resulting fragments gradually expand over the following months. Sexual reproduction occurs on wild reefs through broadcast spawning but is not practiced in the home aquarium.

primary.name: World Wide Corals
primary.notes: Primary trade source. WWC sells Octospawn as a distinct trade category from frogspawn/hammer at premium prices, confirming its market identity as a separate coral. The WWC search page returned 115 product matches for 'octospawn' confirming the trade name is well established; no single dedicated WWC care guide page was accessible, so husbandry parameters were cross-checked across several secondary trade sources listed below.

additional.name: New Dawn Aquaculture — Green Octospawn
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Fimbriaphyllia yaeyamaensis, formerly Euphyllia yaeyamaensis), water parameters (temp 24–26°C, salinity 1.026 SG, pH 8.1–8.4, alk 8 dKH, Ca 440 ppm, Mg 1320 ppm), moderate lighting, low-to-moderate indirect flow, mid-to-lower placement, semi-aggressive temperament, and the distinguishing multi-lobed 'octo' tentacle morphology with neon-green tips on purple/lavender base.

additional.name: ReefSolution — Octospawn Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–26°C / 75–79°F), pH (7.8–8.4), salinity (1.024–1.026 SG), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), calcium (400–450 ppm), magnesium (1250–1450 ppm), PAR (80–150), moderate indirect flow, mid-to-lower placement, 15 cm spacing, intermediate care level, 2–3× weekly feeding preferentially at night.

additional.name: Manta Systems — Ultimate Guide to Euphyllia & Fimbriaphyllia Corals
additional.notes: Cross-checked octospawn's placement within the Fimbriaphyllia genus, temperature 76–80°F, PAR 80–150, moderate-to-gentle flow, supplemental feeding norm, and the standard Euphyllia/Fimbriaphyllia spacing/compatibility guidelines.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Frogspawn Coral, Thick Branched (Euphyllia yaeyamaensis)
additional.notes: Sold under the legacy Euphyllia yaeyamaensis name as 'Thick Branched Frogspawn' rather than 'Octospawn' — but the species is the same. Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025 SG), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), moderate lighting, medium flow, middle placement, aggressive sweeper tentacles extending up to 6 inches (~15 cm) at night, weekly supplemental feeding, family Caryophylliidae (older classification — kept Euphylliidae as the current accepted family per the 2017 Luzon et al. revision). Also corroborates the colony-size grading typical of the trade (small ~4–6 cm up to large ~10–13 cm per head).
